pattern: Sort cars by starting position from closest to the goal and compare arrival times; a trailing car that arrives no later than the leader merges into its fleet, so each strictly slower-arriving leader starts a new fleet.
hints: Order the cars by how far along the road they start, looking from the one nearest the destination backward.
Compute the time each car would take to reach the target on its own; a car behind that arrives no later catches the one ahead and joins its fleet.
Sweep from the front-most car: keep the current fleet's arrival time, and count a new fleet only when a following car's solo arrival time strictly exceeds it.

edge_inputs.description: mixed positions and speeds forming three fleets
single car is its own fleet
successive merges cascade into one fleet
all cars identical speed never catch up, all separate
a faster trailing car is blocked and merges at the target
front car slow, everyone behind piles into one fleet
two independent fleets that do not interact

stress.description: random unique positions and speeds; uniqueness of positions preserved by sampling without replacement
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    target = 1000000
    n = max(1, min(size, target - 1))
    positions = rng.sample(range(0, target), n)
    speed = [rng.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [target, positions, speed]
stress.note: rng.sample guarantees the required uniqueness of positions

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def carFleet(self, target, position, speed):
        # Brute: simulate merges by repeatedly checking arrival times in order.
        cars = sorted(zip(position, speed), reverse=True)
        times = [(target - p) / s for p, s in cars]
        fleets = 0
        i = 0
        n = len(times)
        while i < n:
            fleets += 1
            lead = times[i]
            j = i + 1
            while j < n and times[j] <= lead:
                j += 1
            i = j
        return fleets
